result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant Responder as Responder (xB = B)
    participant Server

    Attacker->>Responder: B
    Attacker->>Responder: encrypt((a3, B), pkB)
    Note over Responder: beginAparam(B,B)
    Responder-->>Attacker: (B, B)

    Attacker->>Server: (a4, B)
    Server-->>Attacker: sign((pkB, B), skS)
    Attacker->>Responder: sign((pkB, B), skS)

    Note over Responder: new Nb
    Responder-->>Attacker: encrypt((a3, Nb, B), pkB)
    Attacker->>Responder: encrypt(a3, pkB)
    Note over Responder: Final check uses NY = a3, not Nb
    Note over Responder: endBparam(B,B) and endBfull(B,B,pkB,pkB,a3,Nb)

    Responder-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretBNa, a3)
    Responder-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretBNb, Nb)
    Note over Attacker: sdecrypt(sencrypt(secretBNa,a3), a3) = secretBNa
result.explanation: ProVerif finds that secretBNa is reachable and that the responder-side authentication queries fail: endBparam/endBfull can occur without matching beginBparam/beginBfull. In the responder, the last check accepts message 7 when it decrypts to NY, the nonce taken from message 3, instead of Nb, the fresh nonce generated by the responder. In the trace, the attacker chooses that nonce value (a3), starts the responder as B, gets B's certificate from the server, and then sends encrypt(a3, pkB) as the last message. This is enough for the responder to complete and output sencrypt(secretBNa, a3), which the attacker can decrypt because a3 is attacker-known.
result.patch_suggestion:
In processResponder, replace the final test so it checks the responder's fresh nonce Nb, using the responder key selected for xB:
if nonce_to_bitstring(Nb) = decrypt(m3, skxB) then
This is the minimal fix directly addressing the shown attack; it makes message 7 prove knowledge of Nb rather than the attacker-chosen NY.
result.notes: The provided counterexample specifically uses xB = B, so the hard-coded use of skB in the buggy check is not what enables this trace; the decisive flaw shown by the trace is checking NY instead of Nb. The .pv file also contains another hard-coded key use in the initiator's decryption step, but that is not exercised by the supplied attack evidence.
